nativeRange.habitat: Warm, shallow, spring-fed pools and outflows of the Diamond Y Spring complex; clear to slightly turbid spring water with aquatic vegetation and algae mats over clay or sandy substrate

decorPreferences: dense low-growing vegetation (Chara, hornwort, or aquatic grasses)
flat rocks or slate for territorial delineation
minimal flow, replicating still spring conditions

diet.notes: Feeds on algae, detritus, small invertebrates, diatoms, and biofilm in the wild. Accepts spirulina flakes, algae wafers, and finely crushed omnivore pellets in captivity. Supplement with live or frozen micro-foods: Artemia nauplii, micro-worms, cyclops, and daphnia. Grazes frequently throughout the day.

goodWith: conspecifics in appropriate male-to-female ratios (1 male : 3+ females)
other non-hybrid Cyprinodon species in managed conservation settings only
avoidWith: Sheepshead Minnow (Cyprinodon variegatus) — hybridization risk is a documented recovery threat
fast-moving or aggressive fish that outcompete for food

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Cyprinodon bovinus. Shows typical adult coloration. Verify availability before use.

summary: A Critically Endangered US endemic pupfish confined to the Diamond Y Spring complex in Pecos County, Texas. Brilliant blue breeding males; ~6 cm. Listed under the US Endangered Species Act (1980). Not legal for private possession — captive conservation breeding programs only.
careNotes:
The Leon Springs Pupfish (Cyprinodon bovinus) is one of North America’s most imperilled freshwater fish, restricted entirely to the Diamond Y Spring complex and associated outflows in Pecos County, Texas. It is listed as Endangered under the US Endangered Species Act since 1980 and is classified as Critically Endangered on the IUCN Red List. Private possession or transport without a US Fish & Wildlife Service permit is a federal violation.

This species exists exclusively in institutional conservation breeding programs — zoos, aquariums, and university research facilities participating in USFWS recovery plans. Husbandry protocols in those settings emphasise genetic management (pedigree tracking to maintain diversity), strict isolation from all other Cyprinodon species to prevent hybridization, and water chemistry closely replicating the alkaline, moderately hard spring water of its native habitat (pH 7.2–8.5, temperature 20–32°C, moderate hardness).

The most critical conservation threat beyond habitat loss is hybridization with the introduced Sheepshead Minnow (C. variegatus), which invaded the Diamond Y system and is capable of producing fertile hybrids, effectively diluting the pure gene pool. Conservation managers have undertaken physical removal of C. variegatus from portions of the spring system and maintain pure-strain populations in isolated refugia tanks.

Breeding males display vivid iridescent blue coloration with orange-edged caudal fins, making them visually striking. Females are drabber with a typical Cyprinodon pattern of brown-grey with dark lateral markings. Males are territorial and will spar with rivals; a strongly skewed female-to-male ratio (3:1 or higher) is recommended in managed populations to reduce male aggression and maximize reproductive output.
breedingNotes: Spawning occurs over sand or fine substrate, with males defending small territories and performing lateral-display courtship. Eggs are deposited singly or in small clutches in substrate or on plant material; no parental care is provided. Incubation takes approximately 7–10 days at 26–28°C. Fry are tiny and require infusoria or commercially prepared fry foods initially, graduating to micro-crushed flake and Artemia nauplii within the first two weeks. Breeding in captivity has been achieved in institutional settings as part of USFWS recovery programs. Genetic provenance tracking is mandatory in all managed breeding efforts.

primary.name: FishBase — Cyprinodon bovinus
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed adult size (to 6.1 cm SL), native range (Diamond Y Spring, Pecos County, Texas), taxonomy (Cyprinodontidae, Cyprinodontiformes), diet (algae, small invertebrates, detritus), temperature range (20–32°C), and IUCN Critically Endangered status. Also cross-referenced IUCN Red List entry and USFWS species profile.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Cyprinodon bovinus
additional.notes: Confirmed Critically Endangered status, primary threats (habitat modification, hybridization with C. variegatus, groundwater extraction), single-site endemism in Diamond Y Spring complex, Pecos County, Texas. Population trend declining.

additional.name: US Fish & Wildlife Service — Leon Springs Pupfish Species Profile
additional.notes: Confirmed ESA listing date (1980), federal protection status, critical habitat designation, and active recovery plan. Possession/transport without permit is a federal violation under 16 U.S.C. § 1538.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Cyprinodon bovinus
additional.notes: Species overview confirming endemic range, ESA listing, hybridization threat from C. variegatus, breeding male coloration (brilliant blue), and conservation-only captive status. Supplemented water parameter and husbandry details.

additional.name: Minckley & Deacon (eds.) — Battle Against Extinction: Native Fish Management in the American West (University of Arizona Press, 1991)
additional.notes: Academic reference for historical range, habitat description, hybridization ecology with C. variegatus, and early captive breeding program context for Leon Springs Pupfish and related Cyprinodon endemics of the Chihuahuan Desert.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeds in captivity under institutional management. Spawns over sand; eggs deposited singly, no parental care. Incubation ~7–10 days at 26–28°C. Genetic pedigree tracking is mandatory in all conservation breeding programs. Not legally bred in private settings.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: US Endangered Species Act listed (1980). Confined to Diamond Y Spring complex in Pecos County, Texas. Possession/transport requires US Fish & Wildlife Service permits. Captive conservation breeding programs only; not legally kept in private hands.
